title: Point Load Test
aim: Point Load Strength Index test and its use to estimate the UCS
theory: Point Load Strength Index test is used to estimate the strength of rock (especially compressive strength) indirectly.
It is an index test, meaning it gives a quick measure rather than exact strength like UCS.
The test is performed by applying load to a rock sample until failure occurs.
Load is applied through conical platens (point contacts) on opposite sides of the specimen.
The failure load (P) is recorded at the moment the rock breaks.
The distance (D) between the contact points is measured.
It is an indirect method used to determine uniaxial compressive strength (UCS) of rock.
Suitable for irregular as well as regular rock samples (unlike UCS which needs perfect specimens).
It is faster, cheaper, and requires less sample preparation compared to UCS test.
A size correction factor is applied when sample diameter is less than standard size (usually 50 mm).
The test is widely used in field and laboratory conditions for rock strength classification.
procedure: Rock samples are selected and checked for cracks or defects.
Suitable specimens (regular or irregular) are prepared and cleaned before testing.
The sample is placed between the two conical platens of the testing machine.
It is positioned such that the load is applied through opposite points of the specimen.
The distance (D) between the contact points is measured using a vernier or dial gauge.
Load is applied gradually and continuously using the loading system.
The load is increased until the sample fails (breaks).
The failure load (P) is recorded from the pressure gauge at the moment of failure.
The same procedure is repeated for multiple samples.
The Point Load Strength Index (Is or ID) is calculated using recorded values.
If required, a size correction factor is applied to standardize results.
Finally, the average value of the index is taken as the result.
result: Point Load Strength of the Sample is 0.7213 KN/cm^2
